title: Unique User Identification — Shared or Generic EHR Accounts
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the HIPAA Security programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with HIPAA Security control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.

compliance.assurance_rationale: HIPAA Security §164.312(a)(2)(i) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that HIPAA Security §164.312(a)(2)(i) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.10.13: Unique User Identification — Shared or Generic EHR Accounts.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.10.13' running on index=epic sourcetype=epic:audit, index=citrix sourcetype=citrix:session, lookup forbidden_shared_accounts.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'Unique User Identification — Shared or Generic EHR Accounts' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper HIPAA Security control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the HIPAA Security control objective.

app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Citrix (2757) (Splunkbase)
spl:
(index=epic sourcetype="epic:audit") OR (index=citrix sourcetype="citrix:session")
| eval account=upper(coalesce(USER_ID, UserName))
| lookup forbidden_shared_accounts.csv account OUTPUT policy
| where isnotnull(policy)
| stats dc(PAT_ID) as patients dc(client_ip) as src_ips count as events by account, _time span=1h
| where events>10
| sort - events
description: Finds authentication and EHR events tied to shared kiosk or interface accounts so every action can be attributed to an identified user as required for ePHI access.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for HIPAA Security compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes citrix, epic and sourcetypes citrix:session, citrix:session`, epic:audit. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Maintain list of prohibited generic accounts; (2) push exceptions only via CAB-approved break-glass IDs; (3) correlate Citrix `ClientName` for kiosk attribution where possible. Indexes required: `index=citrix`, `index=epic`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=citrix:session`, `sourcetype=citrix:session``, `sourcetype=epic:audit`, `sourcetype=epic:audit``; Lookups: `forbidden_shared_accounts`, `forbidden_shared_accounts.csv`.
visualization: Table (account, events, patients), Timeline (shared account spikes).
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Break-the-glass emergency access** — Clinicians exercising documented emergency access procedures to ePHI during patient safety events generate elevated access alerts that are legitimate under the HIPAA treatment, payment, and operations (TPO) exception.

2. **Health information exchange (HIE) activity** — Automated ePHI transmissions through approved health information exchanges and interoperability interfaces produce cross-boundary data flows that are authorised under business associate agreements.

3. **Clinical research data extraction** — IRB-approved research protocols involve de-identified or limited data set extractions that trigger data access monitoring rules but are covered by the approved research data use agreement.

4. **System backup and archival processes** — Scheduled ePHI backup operations, media rotation, and long-term archival procedures generate bulk data access events that are operationally required and documented in the information lifecycle policy.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `hipaa_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved break-glass event IDs, HIE partner identifiers, IRB protocol numbers, and scheduled backup job identifiers. The correlation search filters against this lookup and suppresses matches where `hipaa_exception_approved=true`.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Epic Audit Log Integration (facility-specific TA) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Epic Audit Log Integration (facility-specific TA)" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `epic`, `citrix` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `forbidden_shared_accounts.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('forbidden_shared_accounts.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `§164.312(a)(2)(i)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that HIPAA Security §164.312(a)(2)(i) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.10.13: Unique User Identification — Shared or Generic EHR Accounts." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Epic Audit Log Integration (facility-specific TA) , landing events in `epic`, `citrix` under `epic:audit`, `citrix:session`. Epic Hyperspace audit exports run 5000-50000 events/day depending on facility size; at 1 KB/event plan 5-50 GB/month per facility.

Configure the input through Universal Forwarder on the Epic audit export host; coordinate export schedule with Epic admin team. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[monitor:///opt/epic/audit/export/*.json]
index = epic
sourcetype = epic:audit
disabled = 0
crcSalt = <SOURCE>
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `USER_ID, PAT_ID, AccessType, AccessContext, WorkstationName, Department, _time`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=epic earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


Three operational considerations, authored alongside this UC, apply:


• **Hint 1:** Maintain list of prohibited generic accounts. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** push exceptions only via CAB-approved break-glass IDs. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** correlate Citrix `ClientName` for kiosk attribution where possible. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Unique User Identification — Shared or Generic EHR Accounts," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the HIPAA Security control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `forbidden_shared_accounts.csv` — the identity register that maps accounts to real users and roles. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`policy`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `account`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `forbidden_shared_accounts.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval account = lower(account)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the full search range.

Numeric threshold `events > 10`: this is the noise-to-signal decision point for the detection. Calibrate against your estate's baseline — see the 'Troubleshooting' section below for the calibration query.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.10.13: Unique User Identification — Shared or Generic EHR Accounts]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.10.13,reg=HIPAA_Security
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=epic sourcetype=epic:audit earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `_time`, `account`, `events`, `patients`, `span=1h`, `src_ips`. Run:

```spl
index=epic sourcetype=epic:audit earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table _time, account, events, patients, span=1h, src_ips
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `forbidden_shared_accounts.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup forbidden_shared_accounts.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `events`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(events) as p50 perc90(events) as p90 perc99(events) as p99 max(events) as hi
```

The current threshold `10` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.10.13: HIPAA Security evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`account`, `events`, `patients`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Time trend (`shared account spikes`):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**HIPAA Security-specific failure modes:**

• Minimum-necessary principle: access-anomaly detections (excessive chart views, unexpected departments) must NOT flag clinicians accessing records for treatment purposes — §164.502(b) explicitly permits this. Join detections against the clinician's current department and patient's active encounter to avoid generating thousands of false positives on ED doctors seeing admitted patients.

• Break-glass and emergency access (§164.312(a)(2)(ii)): emergency accounts will legitimately bypass normal access controls during downtime events. Maintain a lookup `breakglass_declarations.csv` written by the clinical informatics on-call; correlate every break-glass access against a declared event — un-declared emergency access is the finding.

• Audit log retention (§164.312(b)): HIPAA requires 6 years of audit log retention, longer than most Splunk index default settings. Verify `frozenTimePeriodInSecs >= 189216000` for every index named in an HIPAA UC's search, and that the archive tier (S3, cold storage) is provisioned.

• Covered vs non-covered entity scope: if the search unintentionally pulls data from a non-covered business unit (e.g. a research arm not operating as a HIPAA Covered Entity), findings are noise. Filter using the facility/cost-center lookup that the Privacy Officer maintains, not by index name alone.
grandmaExplanation: We finds authentication and EHR events tied to shared kiosk or interface accounts so every action can be attributed to an identified user as required for ePHI access. HIPAA is about keeping health records private and secure when we use them in care and operations.
